title: Trauma and Autoimmune Disease: Is There a Connection?
excerpt: Exploring how trauma impacts the immune system and its role in autoimmune diseases. Understand the science behind the mind-body connection.
body:
Introduction: More Than Just Coincidence?

Autoimmune diseases, where the body attacks its own tissues, affect millions worldwide. Conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, and multiple sclerosis can be debilitating and frustratingly complex. Many people with autoimmune disorders also report histories of trauma—whether childhood abuse, neglect, or overwhelming life stressors. But is this connection real or just anecdotal? This article explores the science linking trauma to autoimmune disease, unpacking the biology and offering insight for those seeking deeper understanding and healing.

What Are Autoimmune Diseases?

Autoimmune diseases occur when the immune system malfunctions and mistakenly targets the body’s own cells as foreign invaders. This causes chronic inflammation, tissue damage, and a range of symptoms depending on the organ affected. Common autoimmune diseases include:

  • Rheumatoid arthritis
  • Systemic lupus erythematosus
  • Multiple sclerosis
  • Type 1 diabetes
  • Hashimoto’s thyroiditis

Despite advances, causes remain multi-factorial—comprising genetics, environment, infections, and increasingly recognized, psychosocial factors such as trauma.

Trauma’s Impact on the Nervous System and Immunity

Trauma, especially early-life or chronic trauma, fundamentally alters the nervous system. Bessel van der Kolk, a leader in trauma research, shows how trauma rewires brain circuits responsible for stress regulation and emotional processing (The Body Keeps the Score, 2014). But this rewiring doesn’t stop in the brain—it cascades into the body’s immune system.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how trauma can dysregulate the autonomic nervous system (ANS), shifting the balance between the sympathetic (“fight or flight”) and parasympathetic (“rest and digest”) branches. Chronic activation of the sympathetic nervous system leads to prolonged inflammatory responses.

“Chronic stress and trauma can trigger immune dysregulation, increasing inflammation and potentially contributing to autoimmune disease.” – Stephen Porges

This prolonged inflammation creates a fertile ground for autoimmune reactions, as the immune system becomes hypervigilant and prone to mistakes.

The Science Linking Trauma and Autoimmune Disease

Multiple studies indicate that individuals with autoimmune diseases report higher rates of trauma exposure:

  • A 2017 study in Psychoneuroendocrinology found that women with systemic lupus erythematosus had significantly higher childhood trauma scores compared to controls (Dube et al., 2017).
  • Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, discusses how unresolved trauma leads to chronic nervous system arousal, which can dysregulate immune function (Waking the Tiger, 1997).
  • Research published in Brain, Behavior, and Immunity (2016) confirms that early life adversity correlates with elevated inflammatory markers linked to autoimmune risk (Danese et al., 2016).

These studies do not claim trauma causes autoimmune disease outright, but they strongly suggest trauma acts as a significant risk factor or trigger in genetically predisposed individuals.

How Does Trauma Lead to Immune Dysregulation?

Dr. Gabor Maté, in his work on addiction and chronic illness, emphasizes that trauma imprints on the body’s stress-response systems. Here’s the biological pathway in brief:

  • Stress response activation: Trauma activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and sympathetic nervous system, releasing cortisol and adrenaline.
  • Chronic stress: When stress is constant or unresolved, cortisol receptors become less sensitive, leading to ineffective regulation of inflammation.
  • Inflammation: Immune cells secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines, which can damage tissues and confuse immune targeting.
  • Autoimmunity: This chronic inflammatory environment can precipitate the immune system attacking healthy tissues.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy approach highlights how trauma-induced somatic patterns maintain this dysregulation, creating a feedback loop between body and immune system.

Somatic Healing and Nervous System Regulation: A Path Forward

For those with autoimmune disease and trauma histories, traditional therapies may fall short if they don’t address the body’s role in trauma. Somatic healing approaches focus on:

  • Recalibrating the autonomic nervous system through breath work, movement, and mindfulness
  • Releasing trauma held in the body to reduce chronic stress responses
  • Supporting immune regulation by fostering nervous system safety and balance

Deb Dana, a clinician specializing in Polyvagal Theory, offers practical frameworks for nervous system regulation that can help reduce inflammation and improve quality of life for autoimmune patients.

The Role of Internal Family Systems (IFS) and Trauma Integration

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model provides tools to work with the parts of self impacted by trauma. Integrating trauma in this way can reduce the nervous system’s alarm signals and improve body-mind harmony, indirectly supporting immune function.

By befriending and healing the parts that hold trauma, people can lessen chronic stress and its physiological effects, potentially alleviating autoimmune flares.

Conclusion: Trauma Is a Piece of the Puzzle

The link between trauma and autoimmune disease is complex and nuanced. Trauma doesn’t cause autoimmune disease by itself but can create biological conditions that increase risk or exacerbate symptoms. Understanding this connection opens new paths for healing—ones that honor the body’s wisdom and focus on nervous system regulation.

If you’ve struggled with autoimmune illness and feel stuck despite medical treatment, exploring trauma-informed somatic approaches may be the missing piece. Healing trauma is not just emotional work; it’s a vital part of restoring immune balance and reclaiming health.

References

  • van der Kolk, Bessel. The Body Keeps the Score. 2014.
  • Porges, Stephen. The Polyvagal Theory. 2011.
  • Dube, S. R., et al. "Childhood Trauma and Systemic Lupus Erythematosus." Psychoneuroendocrinology, 2017.
  • Levine, Peter. Waking the Tiger. 1997.
  • Danese, A., et al. "Inflammation and Childhood Adversity." Brain, Behavior, and Immunity, 2016.
  • Maté, Gabor. When the Body Says No. 2003.
  • Ogden, Pat. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. 2006.
  • Dana, Deb. The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. 2018.
  • Schwartz, Richard. Internal Family Systems Therapy. 1995.

faqs.q: Can trauma actually cause autoimmune disease?
faqs.a: Trauma alone does not directly cause autoimmune disease but can increase the risk by dysregulating the nervous and immune systems, especially in those genetically predisposed.

faqs.q: How does nervous system regulation help autoimmune conditions?
faqs.a: Regulating the nervous system reduces chronic stress and inflammation, which can decrease autoimmune flares and improve overall immune function.

faqs.q: Are somatic therapies effective for autoimmune disease?
faqs.a: Somatic therapies can complement medical treatment by addressing trauma-related nervous system dysregulation, potentially improving symptoms and quality of life.

faqs.q: What role does early life trauma play in autoimmune disorders?
faqs.a: Early life trauma is strongly linked to long-term immune dysregulation and inflammation, which can predispose individuals to developing autoimmune diseases later in life.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma reshapes the body and mind, essential for understanding trauma's impact on health.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Provides practical tools for nervous system regulation, a key to managing trauma and its physiological effects.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Introduces somatic experiencing and explains the biological basis of trauma and healing.

metaTitle: Trauma and Autoimmune Disease: Exploring the Connection
metaDescription: Discover the science linking trauma to autoimmune disease and how somatic healing supports nervous system regulation.
author: The Oracle Lover
